paragraphs.body: NVIDIA and Apple NVIDIA is a company that produces GPUs (graphics cards), which are components found in computers that help with gaming, 3D rendering, AI language model training, video editing, and more. With the recent boom in AI, AI startup companies require graphics cards in bulk. Since NVIDIA is the only company that makes cards good enough for this market, NVIDIA’s stock soared to become one of the biggest in the world. Their focus shifted from making cards designated towards gamers to cards made for AI-related workloads, and this was abundantly clear in the new generation of cards NVIDIA recently released.

Ignoring the low supply of the 5080 and looking purely at the card itself, it has proven to be a disappointingly low improvement over its last-generation counterpart, the 4080 super. In, “NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5080 review” by Gamers Nexus, they do a thorough analysis of this card, and one of their points that sticks out is the unremarkable increase in raw performance from generation to generation. They point out, “The 5080 is a relatively boring uplift over the 4080, unfortunately. This must be why NVIDIA has pushed MFG so hard” (Note: MFG stands for Multi-Frame generation. I will go more in-depth on what this is later). These findings by Gamers Nexus and similar journalists contribute to the point that NVIDIA relies on its reputation rather than making good products. Despite the 50 series of cards using a brand new architecture called “Blackwell architecture” by NVIDIA, they cannot seem to improve their latest generation significantly. While testing the 5080 versus its previous-gen counterparts as well as some competitors, Gamers Nexus found the 5080 to be on par with some of its cheaper competition and only 10-20% better than the 4080 super. NVIDIA knew that people wouldn’t be happy about the lackluster improvements, so the marketing surrounding the 5080 (and all 50 series cards) was entirely spent highlighting new upscaling technologies, those being DLSS 4 and MFG. DLSS 4 (deep learning super sampling) is the 4th version of DLSS, it renders games at lower resolutions and then uses AI to predict what the game should look like at higher resolutions. The result is getting better performance at the cost of more input lag as well as some graphical glitches. MFG is similar, but it creates “AI frames” that increase the frame rate of the games you play, at the cost of significant input lag. Due to NVIDIA using these technologies to justify their ridiculous prices and create deceiving marketing, most consumers are not huge fans of these technologies. I can speak from personal experience that Frame generation (the lesser version of MFG) had a ton of input lag and made playing games not enjoyable.

annotations.comment: Nice job with summary--it's difficult to "translate" technical language and jargon, but you're doing a great job :)
annotations.context_left: generation (the lesser version of MFG) had a ton of input lag and made playing games not enjoyable.

annotations.comment: I wonder, though, how much of this summary is necessary to "point" your reader to what's really at the heart of this essay? Remember that you only need enough summary to provide some context and background for your own ideas, which are the most important part of this essay as a whole.
annotations.context_left: generation (the lesser version of MFG) had a ton of input lag and made playing games not enjoyable.

paragraphs.body: The one thing the 5080 did right was power efficiency. This was another metric that Gamers Nexus pointed out in their review of the card. In their review, they highlight, “Final Fantasy 14 at 4K had the RTX 5080 at the top of the chart for efficiency. Comparatively, this is a great result.” While Gamers Nexus does not say it in their article, power efficiency is one of the least important factors when considering if a GPU is worth it or not, and the fact that this is the biggest draw of the card highlights how mediocre it is. This is especially true in the US, where most consumers are more concerned with the performance compared to the price of the card. However, in countries or just anywhere where electricity is a lot more expensive, having more efficient electronics becomes imperative to avoid $500+ electricity bills. For people in such situations, it might make sense to buy a card like this.

annotations.context_right: While Gamers Nexus does not s

paragraphs.body: In middle school when I got my first “phone” (it was an iPod), I was super thankful, as a lot of my classmates have had one for a long time. I’ve had a few replacements every 5 years or so, and so has my family. One thing has remained consistent for every phone me and my family have gotten: they were all iPhones (or at least made by Apple).

Ever since I got into PC hardware as a hobby, I’ve also learned a few things about the hardware inside of phones. About 2 years ago, I had an iPhone 7 along with most of my family, and my mom decided it was time for us to upgrade. When I heard about this, I chimed in and suggested that maybe we should consider getting an Android or some other phone made by a different company. My sister was close by and heard my suggestion, in response she said something along the lines of “Ew, who would ever want to get an android? Those are terrible!”. I caved and we all ended up getting iPhone 14s. One day I was curious about the hardware inside of my iPhone 14, and how it compares to other iPhones. As it turns out, the only discernable difference between the iPhone14 and iPhone13 was the camera, and it even does some things worse like not having a SIM card tray.

Seeing this lack of innovation, you may ask yourself “Why do consumers buy this crap?” Indeed, that is what I was asking myself, thinking of the hundreds of thousands of people who fork over nearly $1,000 every time a new generation of phones comes out. I then remembered back to how dogmatically against Androids my sister was. Despite my vastly superior knowledge of technical hardware, she would not budge. This phenomenon is well known, and it’s called brand loyalty. Instead of consumers looking at all of their options from a logical standpoint, they stick to one brand and no matter how shitty the product is, the consumers will buy it. Why? Simply because research requires brain power and time. Your average consumer does not want to or can’t pour hours into learning the intricacies of a given product and then researching how different options compare.

Apple is by far the most popular example of brand loyalty, and even I am technically a victim of it. Despite other companies offering better or on-par products, Apple still manages to outcompete them all due to taking advantage of its loyal customers. Another popular example of brand loyalty is NVIDIA. The launch of the 5080, despite all its flaws and disappointments, saw almost all of its supply selling out (while it's true that 5080s being sold out is due to low supply and not incredibly high demand, even if the supply was large it’s likely NVIDIA would still sell a ton of them). NVIDIA’s rise to becoming a tech giant is parallel to Apple, both companies made great products at first, then using their reputation they are able to coast and beat out their competitors while churning out subpar products.

annotations.text: Apple is by far the most popular example of brand loyalty, and even I am technically a victim of it.
annotations.comment: Yes. Interesting. I wonder, though, what is the "so what?" of this idea for your essay? Who is your essay's audience? What is your essay's purpose?

paragraphs.body: There are countless examples of companies who, intentionally or unintentionally, abuse the brand loyalty that their customers have. Brand loyalty, as we have seen with iPhones and NVIDIA GPUs leads to overpricing and a lack of innovation, both of which negatively impact the consumer experience. That being said, I can sit here all day and complain about how companies take advantage of us but at the end of the day, it's the consumers who perpetuate this phenomenon. So what can you as a consumer, whether it be phones, GPUs, or some other product do to prevent yourself from falling into this trap? There is the obvious answer of doing your research before buying, but asking people to do that for everything they buy is unrealistic, as research requires a lot of time. If you are willing to like I am, that’s the best solution but I believe there is a better one for the average person: don’t replace your product every generation. A lot of people, myself included, already do this, but there are even more people who buy every new generation as I mentioned with iPhones earlier. If you wait 3,4 or even 5 generations before buying a new product, whatever new product you get will be a big improvement over what you had previously even if what you got isn’t a big difference from the previous generation. Another potential solution is when you get a new product, don’t get rid of your old one. Compare the two, whether it be side by side or back to back. If you can’t find a difference that matters to you, take note and either return your new product or just keep that in mind for when the next generation comes around. We can’t all be experts in everything, but it is our duty as customers to keep these companies in check, as you deserve their best.

annotations.text: If you wait 3,4 or even 5 generations before buying a new product, whatever new product you get will be a big improvement over what you had previously even if what you got isn’t a big difference from the previous generation.
annotations.comment: Interesting! This is an effective use of an appeal to logic.

paragraphs.body: Let's say you don't buy any of the completely valid reasons listed. Why does caring about brand loyalty matter? Look at this from a big-picture perspective. You are letting society dictate your spending habits, and it’s not like society does so with an impartial view. I know that I value my and others' individuality a lot, and I don’t want any group, no matter who, telling me what to do. Even if you are someone who believes that the government or some other body should control all aspects of our lives for the sake of efficiency or whatever it might be, you must recognize that individuality is important to have. When we think of who invented the lightbulb, does Thomas Edison come to mind, or his company? It's true that groups of people can achieve great things, but at the end of the day it's the individuals who are actually doing things. If you want to sink in the mud along with most other people, that’s fine by me but I’m going to live my life how I want to, and make decisions for myself that I know/believe are best for me.

annotations.text: You are letting society dictate your spending habits, and it’s not like society does so with an impartial view. I know that I value my and others' individuality a lot, and I don’t want any group, no matter who, telling me what to do.
